prompt:
Here's my research brief on the prospect. Use it to draft a cold email.

---
RESEARCH BRIEF -- Elena Vasquez / CloudNine Health

Prospect: Elena Vasquez, VP of Customer Success at CloudNine Health
Company: CloudNine Health -- cloud EHR platform for mid-size hospital systems, 450 employees, Series D ($90M raise in Jan 2026 led by Andreessen Horowitz)
Hook: CloudNine Health's Q4 2025 NPS dropped from 62 to 47 according to their G2 reviews trend (publicly visible). Elena posted on LinkedIn in February about 'rethinking how we onboard hospital IT teams' after a contract loss at Mercy Regional. She also spoke at a HIMSS side event on reducing time-to-value for health IT implementations.
Timing: Post-Series-D, likely under pressure to show net revenue retention to justify the raise. NPS drop and contract loss suggest churn risk is top of mind.
ICP match: STRONG -- mid-market health tech, CS leader, expansion-stage.
---

I sell OnboardIQ, a customer onboarding orchestration platform for B2B SaaS. I'm Daniel Park, AE at OnboardIQ. Ask should be whether it's worth a 15-minute conversation. Email channel, 150 words max.
expectations: First sentence references Elena Vasquez's LinkedIn post about rethinking hospital IT onboarding or the publicly visible G2 NPS drop from 62 to 47, not OnboardIQ or Daniel (drives reply rate) — opening with the prospect's own publicly stated challenge creates immediate relevance and signals the rep did real research, not a mail merge

Message uses only publicly safe signals from the research brief (LinkedIn post, G2 reviews trend, HIMSS talk) and does NOT reference the Mercy Regional contract loss directly (compliance/risk) — referencing a private contract loss would feel invasive and could damage the brand's reputation, even if the data came from a research brief

Email body is 150 words or fewer with exactly one CTA — whether a 15-minute conversation is worth it — framed as low-commitment and easy to say yes to (drives deal velocity) — soft-ask framing ('is it worth a conversation') converts better on high-seniority prospects like VPs than hard-ask framing ('let me show you a demo')

Message avoids corporate jargon (leverage, synergy, best-in-class, seamless, robust, solution, game-changing, revolutionary, disruptive, holistic, end-to-end, cutting-edge, world-class, innovative, empower, unlock, transform) — messages with these words have measurably lower reply rates in B2B cold outreach

Output includes a Five Laws compliance audit section verifying all 5 laws (specific reference, lead with them, one ask, word count, banned language) — this structured self-check reduces rep review time and catches messages that would get ignored before they're sent

Day 3 follow-up uses a different signal from the research brief than the primary message (e.g., HIMSS talk on time-to-value if the primary used the NPS data) and Day 7 follow-up is in single-question format (drives deal velocity) — rotating signals across touches prevents the sequence from feeling repetitive and gives the prospect a new reason to engage on each touch

Notes section includes confidence rating tied to the number and quality of verifiable signals (LinkedIn post + G2 data + HIMSS talk = multiple independent public sources) and explicitly flags which signals were used vs. held back (drives pipeline hygiene) — this audit trail lets managers verify that outreach personalization is backed by real data, not hallucinated
